chapter: Chapter 5: Pastoralists in the Modern World

questions.question: The seasonal movement of the Gujjar Bakarwals is defined by which of the following cycles?

options.A: Moving to the Bhabar in summer and Bugyals in winter
options.B: Moving to the high mountains (Bugyals) in summer and low hills (Shiwalik) in winter
options.C: Moving from the desert to the coastal plains during monsoon
options.D: Staying in the plains during the rainy season and mountains during dry spells

questions.explanation: In winter, when the high mountains are covered with snow, they move to the low dry forests of the Shiwalik hills; by April, they begin their northern march to the high summer pastures.

questions.question: What specifically caused the 'Dhangars' of Maharashtra to leave the central plateau every year around October?

options.A: The onset of heavy snowfall
options.B: The shortage of forage due to the semi-arid nature of the plateau
options.C: The arrival of the Konkani peasants to claim the land
options.D: Government orders to return to the mountains

questions.explanation: The central plateau of Maharashtra is a semi-arid region with low rainfall; by October, the Dhangars move west toward the Konkan to find better grazing.

questions.question: The relationship between the Dhangars and the Konkani peasants is an example of 'mutual benefit' because:

options.A: Dhangars provided labor in exchange for rice
options.B: Dhangar flocks manured the fields and ate the stubble, while peasants gave them supplies of rice
options.C: The Konkani peasants herded the Dhangar cattle during monsoon
options.D: The Dhangars protected the Konkan coast from foreign invaders

questions.explanation: After the kharif harvest, the fields needed to be fertilized for the rabi crop; the Dhangar flocks provided this manure.

questions.question: How did the 'Waste Land Rules' change the status of pastoralists in the eyes of the British?

options.A: They were recognized as the rightful owners of the land
options.B: Their grazing lands were seen as 'unproductive' because they did not produce land revenue
options.C: They were encouraged to plant commercial crops on the waste land
options.D: They were given subsidies to maintain the pastures

questions.explanation: The British wanted to expand cultivation to increase revenue, and since pastures produced no tax or commercial agricultural products, they were labeled as 'waste'.

questions.question: Under the Forest Acts, the British classified some forests as 'Reserved' specifically to grow:

options.A: Fruit trees for the local markets
options.B: Timber like Deodar and Sal for the railways
options.C: Grasses for the pastoralists' herds
options.D: Flowers for the botanical gardens in London

questions.explanation: The primary goal of forest reservation was to secure a steady supply of high-quality timber for the Royal Navy and the growing railway network.

questions.question: The Criminal Tribes Act (1871) primarily aimed to:

options.A: Stop the spread of diseases in nomadic herds
options.B: Force nomadic people into settled life to make them easier to monitor and tax
options.C: Protect nomads from the attacks of settled farmers
options.D: Grant special trading rights to the Raikas

questions.explanation: The British were suspicious of mobile people who could not be easily located or identified; they preferred a settled population for administrative control.

questions.question: How did the 'Grazing Tax' collection system change in the mid-19th century?

options.A: It was abolished to help the poor
options.B: The right to collect tax was auctioned out to contractors
options.C: It was paid in wool instead of cash
options.D: Only the village headmen were allowed to collect it

questions.explanation: The government auctioned the right to collect taxes to contractors who tried to extract as much as possible from the pastoralists.

questions.question: What was the long-term ecological impact of restricting pastoralists to smaller, fixed areas of land?

options.A: The soil became more fertile due to less use
options.B: Continuous grazing on the same patch led to the decline of pasture quality
options.C: The grass grew faster and thicker
options.D: Natural forests began to expand into the grazing zones

questions.explanation: Normally, movement allowed pastures to recover; when movement was restricted, the grazing pressure on single patches led to deterioration and forage shortages.

questions.question: The division of Maasailand in 1885 resulted in the Maasai losing approximately what percentage of their grazing land?

options.A: 10 per cent
options.B: 60 per cent
options.C: 25 per cent
options.D: 90 per cent

questions.explanation: The Maasai were pushed into a small area in south Kenya and north Tanzania, losing about 60% of their pre-colonial lands.

questions.question: In the pre-colonial period, the 'Elders' of Maasai society were responsible for:

options.A: Raiding cattle from neighboring tribes
options.B: Meeting in councils to decide on tribal affairs and settle disputes
options.C: Tending the sheep and goats in the mountains
options.D: Trading with the European merchants

questions.explanation: The Elders formed the ruling body that managed the social and legal aspects of the community through regular councils.

questions.question: How did the colonial policy of appointing 'Chiefs' affect Maasai social structure?

options.A: It strengthened the traditional power of the Elders
options.B: It created a new distinction between wealthy pastoralist-chiefs and poor herders
options.C: It abolished the role of the Warriors
options.D: It made the community entirely democratic

questions.explanation: Appointed chiefs often accumulated wealth through trade and land, while regular herders remained dependent on their livestock, which were vulnerable to climate shocks.

questions.question: The 'Raika' community of Rajasthan adapted to the dry season by:

options.A: Building massive underground water tanks
options.B: Moving out of their villages in October in search of new pastures and returning in the monsoon
options.C: Stopping the herding of camels and focusing only on sheep
options.D: Moving to the high mountains of the Himalayas

questions.explanation: The Raikas utilized the monsoon at home but migrated when the desert vegetation was exhausted.

questions.question: British officials believed that 'nomadism' was a sign of:

options.A: A highly advanced economy
options.B: A primitive state of society that needed to be 'civilized' and settled
options.C: Environmental protection
options.D: Military strength

questions.explanation: The colonial mindset viewed settled agriculture as the only 'civilized' form of land use and revenue generation.

questions.question: What happened to the pastoralists when the partition of India occurred in 1947?

options.A: They were given free grazing rights in both countries
options.B: Routes were blocked, forcing communities like the Raikas to find new pastures within India (e.g., Haryana)
options.C: They were forced to give up their animals and work in factories
options.D: They became the leaders of the new governments

questions.explanation: Political boundaries, like those created in 1947, disrupted traditional migration routes to the Indus valley, forcing herders to find new grazing lands in places like Haryana.

questions.question: In Maasai society, the 'Warriors' traditionally gained status by:

options.A: Collecting the most taxes for the British
options.B: Defending the community and proving their bravery through cattle raids
options.C: Studying at the colonial mission schools
options.D: Marrying the daughters of the appointed Chiefs

questions.explanation: Raiding was a way of asserting the strength of a tribe and was a crucial part of becoming a man and a warrior.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the 'Bhabar' region?

options.A: High altitude meadows covered in flowers
options.B: Dry forested area at the foothills of the mountains
options.C: The swampy lands of the Konkan coast
options.D: The sandy dunes of the Thar desert

questions.explanation: Bhabar is the belt of dry forest at the base of the mountains where pastoralists find shelter and forage in the winter.

questions.question: What was the 'unintended consequence' of the British creating National Parks in Africa?

options.A: The animals died out because there were no people
options.B: Pastoralists were excluded from their best grazing lands, leading to a crisis in their economy
options.C: The parks became too small for the lions
options.D: The pastoralists became world-famous tour guides

questions.explanation: National parks like Serengeti were carved out of the prime grazing lands of the Maasai, severely impacting their survival strategies.

questions.question: The Gaddi shepherds of Himachal Pradesh spend their winters in:

options.A: The high Bugyals
options.B: The low hills of Shiwalik
options.C: The plains of Punjab
options.D: The desert of Rajasthan

questions.explanation: Like the Bakarwals, the Gaddis move to the lower Shiwalik range to escape the freezing winter snows.

questions.question: Why did many Dhangar pastoralists keep 'buffaloes' only in the central plateau and not move them to the Konkan?

options.A: Buffaloes were afraid of the sea
options.B: The wet conditions of the Konkan during monsoon were unsuitable for them
options.C: The Konkani peasants did not like buffalo milk
options.D: Buffaloes were too slow to make the long journey

questions.explanation: While some Dhangars moved, the environment of the coastal Konkan was not healthy for all types of livestock during the heavy rains.

questions.question: The 'Kafila' refers to:

options.A: A special tax on sheep
options.B: The group of households traveling together during migration
options.C: The high-quality wool obtained from mountain goats
options.D: A type of grass found in the Bugyals

questions.explanation: Gujjar Bakarwals move in groups called kafilas to ensure safety and cooperation during their long journeys.

questions.question: How did the colonial government's 'permit system' affect pastoralists' movements?

options.A: It allowed them to move faster
options.B: It restricted the number of days they could spend in a forest and monitored their entry/exit
options.C: It gave them free access to all reserved forests
options.D: It required them to wear a specific uniform

questions.explanation: Permits were a tool of control, strictly limiting the time and area of grazing to prevent what the British saw as 'overuse' of the forest.

questions.question: During a drought, why do Maasai cattle die in large numbers?

options.A: Because they are not allowed to move to better pastures in National Parks or private farms
options.B: Because they have no water to drink
options.C: Because the lions in the parks eat them
options.D: Because the appointed Chiefs steal the cattle

questions.explanation: Traditionally, nomads survive drought by moving; colonial and modern restrictions on movement make these natural cycles deadly.

questions.question: The 'Gollas' of Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh are known for herding:

options.A: Camels
options.C: Sheep and goats
options.D: Horses

questions.explanation: The Gollas herded cattle, while the Kurumas and Kurubas in the same region herded sheep and goats.

questions.question: Rich pastoralists often coped with colonial restrictions by:

options.A: Leading a revolution against the British
options.B: Settling down and buying land for cultivation and trade
options.C: Moving to the cities to work in factories
options.D: Giving all their animals to the poor

questions.explanation: Wealthier herders had the capital to transition into a settled lifestyle, often becoming landlords or traders.

questions.question: What is the status of nomadic pastoralism in the world today according to the text?

options.A: It has been completely abolished by all governments
options.B: It is recognized by many as a sensible way to live in dry and mountainous regions
options.C: It is only practiced in the Serengeti National Park
options.D: It is the main reason for global warming

questions.explanation: Many environmentalists and geographers now realize that nomadic movement is a highly adapted and sustainable way of life for fragile environments.
